title: CSS for Print Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of the essential properties and tools for creating professional-looking print layouts for your website using CSS.

questions.question: Which of these is the most modern and recommended way to build a professional, responsive web page for the physical world?
options: Table Layouts
Float Layouts
The @media print media query
Absolute Positioning only

questions.question: Which of these is a major 'Pro' of using Print-specific CSS styles compared to a regular website?
options: It makes your CSS files smaller automatically.
It allows users to print only the core content (like an article or receipt) while hiding non-essential sidebars and navbars.
It is always supported by every browser.
It replaces the need for any HTML5 tags.

questions.question: What is the primary role of 'break-before' or 'break-after' properties in a print-specific CSS rule?
options: To make the site load much faster.
To control exactly where the printer starts a new page of content.
To choose the colors for the animation.
To automatically stop the animation halfway.

questions.question: How can you show the actual URL (href) of a link when it is printed on physical paper?
options: By writing it out by hand.
Using the CSS '::after' pseudo-element and the 'attr(href)' function.
By taking a screenshot of the link.
You cannot show a URL when printing.

questions.question: Which of these is a BEST practice for a print-style stylesheet to avoid wasting user ink/toner?
options: Making the background black and text white.
Making the background white and the text black.
Using large, dark background images.
Using many different fonts.
